返回本批次

阿耳忒弥斯二号的厕所是月球任务的里程碑

Artemis II's toilet is a moon mission milestone

www.scientificamerican.com 作者 1659447091 分数 146 评论 61 2026/04/02 10:37
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 在前往月球的旅程中，美国宇航局的宇航员终于获得了一些陆地厕所的物质享受，比如有一扇门，能够同时撒尿和大便。美国宇航局阿耳忒弥斯二号任务的月球宇航员将大胆地前往以前从未去过的地方，这要归功于该宇航局首次将功能性厕所绕月飞行。美国宇航局/比尔·英格尔斯美国宇航局已将四名宇航员送入月球，开启了一项开创性的绕月之旅——阿耳忒弥斯二号任务。请关注我们的报道。当宇航员第一次登上月球时，他们没有厕所。根据美国宇航局随后的一份报告，阿波罗计划的塑料袋和漏斗系统非常笨重和混乱，以至于机组人员认为它“令人反感”和“令人厌恶”。但现在，距离上一次载人登月和厕所问题已经过去了半个多世纪，美国宇航局阿耳忒弥斯二号任务的四名宇航员将带着更宽敞的浴室起飞。航天局的通用废物管理系统（UWMS）——更通俗地称为“厕所”——是为了解决宇航员长期面临的如厕问题，并在最后的边境提供更熟悉的浴室体验。月球宇航员现在将被各种便利设施所宠坏，其中包括帮助他们在微重力下保持稳定的把手、可以同时处理尿液和粪便的系统、适用于男性和女性宇航员的尿液收集装置，甚至还有一扇门，可以在狭窄的乘员舱中营造出一种有益的隐私幻觉。新设计已经酝酿了十多年。太空基础设施公司柯林斯航空航天公司 (Collins Aerospace) 于 2015 年首次与 NASA 签订了开发该项目的合同。当时，项目科学家们克服了过去太空厕所的基本问题，同时想象并满足未来的需求，以便阿耳忒弥斯二号宇航员使用的同一系统可以在未来几十年内适应月球和火星任务。关于支持科学新闻如果您喜欢这篇文章，请考虑通过订阅来支持我们屡获殊荣的新闻。通过购买订阅，您将有助于确保有关塑造当今世界的发现和想法的影响力故事的未来。“我认为废物管理是设计的演变，”NASA UWMS 团队的项目经理兼首席研究员 Melissa McKinley 说。 “这款厕所是建立在阿波罗号、航天飞机甚至国际空间站的设计基础上的……里面有很多学问。”在阿波罗号乘员舱的狭小空间里，宇航员每次需要排便或小便时，都会把边缘粘着的塑料袋和管子绑在身上。在失重条件下，安装笨拙的袋子是非常困难的，但宇航员还必须手动混合一包杀菌剂，以防止密封袋内细菌和气体的积聚。该系统因容易泄漏而臭名昭著，例如在阿波罗 10 号任务期间，宇航员注意到“空中漂浮着一团粪便”，在阿波罗 8 号任务期间，宇航员必须追捕逃入机舱的呕吐物和粪便。阿波罗任务结束后发布的一份 NASA 报告指出，就宇航员满意度而言，废物处理“必须给予低分”。“我曾经想成为第一个登上火星的人，”阿波罗 16 号任务期间，宇航员肯·马丁利 (Ken Mattingly) 在描述该系统后说道。 “这让我相信，如果我们要继续阿波罗计划，我就不感兴趣。”根据这些严厉的评论，美国宇航局的科学家知道他们必须创建一个更精简的系统。毕竟，“厕所是一个‘关键任务’系统，所以如果它发生故障，整个任务就会处于危险之中，”纽约城市大学的科学技术历史学家戴维·穆恩斯 (David Munns) 说。一个特殊的月球版本将陪伴航天局的阿耳忒弥斯宇航员乘坐猎户座飞船前往月球。NASA/JSC/James Blair因此，在航天飞机计划之前，他们设计了一种可以在低重力环境下工作的马桶。它看起来很像一个典型的地面厕所，但要求宇航员系好安全带并使用真空软管，以防止废物漂浮回航天器中。航天飞机和国际空间站 (ISS) 上的早期厕所都使用这种真空系统，主要区别在于国际空间站模型回收了一些废水，而航天飞机版本则将其排放到太空中。与阿波罗时代的“厕所”相比，这两个系统都得到了显着改进，但仍然存在很大的局限性。它们在建造时并没有考虑到女性的解剖结构，无法同时处理尿液和粪便，虽然它们用窗帘提供了一些表面上的隐私，但还没有坚固的门。 UWMS 是所有这些被压抑的航空航天工程的顶峰用户体验问题。采用钛 3D 打印而成，其轻质、标准化设计意味着它可以轻松安装在许多不同类型的航天器中，包括国际空间站、阿尔忒弥斯任务的猎户座太空舱以及尚未建造的潜在未来飞行器。UWMS 的第一个版本于 2020 年在国际空间站上进行了测试，最终安装于 2021 年完成。它的特点是可以同时使用尿液和粪便系统，并进行了修改以使这些系统更加男女通用，以及令人垂涎的浴室门。为了帮助同一系统在月球任务中发挥作用，UWMS 的一个版本也被安装在阿耳忒弥斯二号的猎户座太空舱中，这是该计划的首次载人发射，而 UWMS 项目的科学家们正兴奋不已，渴望了解该任务的四名宇航员是否对该设计感到满意。“我很高兴机组人员能够使用它，”麦金利说。 “当这次任务回来时，我们会知道更多......这确实会推动未来阿耳忒弥斯任务和月球活动以及即将到来的火星活动的[废物管理]。”是时候为科学挺身而出了如果您喜欢这篇文章，我想请求您的支持。 180 年来，《科学美国人》一直是科学和工业的倡导者，现在可能是这两个世纪历史中最关键的时刻。我从 12 岁起就成为《科学美国人》的订阅者，它帮助塑造了我看待世界的方式。 SciAm 总是给我带来教育和快乐，并激发我对广阔而美丽的宇宙的敬畏之情。我希望它也能为您做到这一点。如果您订阅《科学美国人》，您将有助于确保我们的报道以有意义的研究和发现为中心；我们有资源来报告威胁美国各地实验室的决定；在科学本身的价值常常被忽视的时代，我们为崭露头角和在职科学家提供支持。作为回报，您可以获得重要新闻、引人入胜的播客、精彩的信息图表、不容错过的时事通讯、必看视频、具有挑战性的游戏以及科学界最好的写作和报告。您甚至可以向某人赠送订阅。现在是我们站起来展示科学重要性的重要时刻。我希望您能支持我们完成这一使命。

Original Summary

  1. On their voyages to the moon, NASA’s astronauts are finally getting some creature comforts of terrestrial toilets—such as having a door and being able to pee and poop simultaneouslyBy K. R. Callaway edited by Lee BillingsThe lunar-bound astronauts of NASA’s Artemis II mission will go boldly where none have gone before, thanks to the space agency’s first-ever flight of a functional toilet around the moon. NASA/Bill IngallsNASA has launched four astronauts on a pioneering journey around the moon—the Artemis II mission. Follow our coverage here.When astronauts first made their way to the moon, they did so without a toilet. The Apollo program’s system of plastic bags and funnels was so unwieldy and messy that crew members found it “objectionable” and “distasteful,” according to a subsequent NASA report. But now, more than a half century since the last crewed lunar voyages and their toilet troubles, the four astronauts of NASA’s Artemis II mission will take flight with a more commodious bathroom in tow.The space agency’s Universal Waste Management System (UWMS)—more colloquially called just “the toilet”—was created to solve longstanding potty problems faced by astronauts and to offer a more familiar bathroom experience on the final frontier. Lunar astronauts will now be spoiled by amenities that include handles to help them stay steady in microgravity, a system that can handle both urine and feces simultaneously, urine-collection devices that work for both male and female astronauts, and even a door for the helpful illusion of privacy in a cramped crew capsule.The new design is more than a decade in the making. Space infrastructure company Collins Aerospace first entered into a contract with NASA to develop the project in 2015. In that time, project scientists have overcome fundamental issues with past space toilets while imagining and meeting future needs so that the same system used by Artemis II astronauts could be adapted for moon and Mars missions in decades to come.On supporting science journalismIf you're enjoying this article, consider supporting our award-winning journalism by subscribing. By purchasing a subscription you are helping to ensure the future of impactful stories about the discoveries and ideas shaping our world today.“I think of waste management as an evolution of design,” says Melissa McKinley, project manager and principal investigator for NASA’s UWMS team. “The toilet has built on designs from Apollo, the space shuttle and even the International Space Station.... There is so much learning that goes into it.”In the tight quarters of Apollo crew capsules, astronauts strapped adhesive-rimmed plastic bags and tubes to themselves whenever they had to defecate or urinate. Attaching the awkward bags was difficult enough in weightless conditions, but the astronauts also had to manually mix in a packet of germicide to prevent the buildup of bacteria and gases within the sealed bag.The system was infamously prone to leaks, such as during the Apollo 10 mission, when astronauts noticed “a turd floating through the air,” and during the Apollo 8 mission, when the crew had to chase down blobs of vomit and feces that escaped into the cabin. A NASA report released after the end of the Apollo missions noted that waste disposal “must be given poor marks” when it comes to crew satisfaction.“I used to want to be the first man to Mars,” said astronaut Ken Mattingly during the Apollo 16 mission, after describing the system. “This has convinced me that, if we got to go on Apollo, I ain’t interested.”Based on these scathing reviews, NASA scientists knew they had to create a more streamlined system. After all, “the toilet is a ‘mission-critical’ system, so if it breaks down, the whole mission is in jeopardy,” says David Munns, a science and technology historian at the City University of New York.This version of NASA’s Universal Waste Management System was sent to the International Space Station; a special lunar version will accompany the space agency’s Artemis astronauts onboard Orion spacecraft bound for the moon.NASA/JSC/James BlairSo before the space shuttle program, they engineered a toilet that could work in a low-gravity environment. It looked much like a typical terrestrial toilet but required the astronauts to strap in and use a vacuum hose to prevent waste from floating back up into the spacecraft.Early toilets on both the space shuttle and the International Space Station (ISS) used this vacuum system—with the key difference being that the ISS model recycled some wastewater, whereas the space shuttle’s version vented it into space. Both systems were significantly improved over the “toilets” of the Apollo years but still had big limitations. They weren’t built with female anatomy in mind and couldn’t process urine and feces at the same time, and while they provided some semblance of privacy with a curtain, there wasn’t yet a solid door.The UWMS is the aerospace-engineered culmination of all these pent-up problems with the user experience. 3D-printed from titanium, its lightweight, standardized design means it can easily fit in many different types of spacecraft, including the ISS, the Artemis missions’ Orion capsule and potential future vehicles that have yet to be built.The first version of the UWMS was tested on the ISS in 2020, and final installation was completed in 2021. It featured urine and feces systems that could be used simultaneously, modifications to make these systems more unisex and the much-coveted bathroom door. With further modifications to help the same system function on a lunar mission, a version of the UWMS has also been installed in the Orion capsule for Artemis II, the program’s first crewed launch—and UWMS project scientists are on the edge of their seats, eager to learn whether the mission’s four astronauts are happy with the design.“I am very excited for the crew to use this,” McKinley says. “We’ll know so much more when this mission comes back.... It’s really going to drive [waste management] on future Artemis missions and the lunar campaign—as well as the Mars campaign to come.”It’s Time to Stand Up for ScienceIf you enjoyed this article, I’d like to ask for your support. Scientific American has served as an advocate for science and industry for 180 years, and right now may be the most critical moment in that two-century history.I’ve been a Scientific American subscriber since I was 12 years old, and it helped shape the way I look at the world. SciAm always educates and delights me, and inspires a sense of awe for our vast, beautiful universe. I hope it does that for you, too.If you subscribe to Scientific American, you help ensure that our coverage is centered on meaningful research and discovery; that we have the resources to report on the decisions that threaten labs across the U.S.; and that we support both budding and working scientists at a time when the value of science itself too often goes unrecognized.In return, you get essential news, captivating podcasts, brilliant infographics, can't-miss newsletters, must-watch videos, challenging games, and the science world's best writing and reporting. You can even gift someone a subscription.There has never been a more important time for us to stand up and show why science matters. I hope you’ll support us in that mission.

评论（默认中文）

0x38B 2026/04/03 05:52
更多关于宇航员认为阿波罗系统“令人反感”和“令人反感”的信息，请参阅 OP (1) 中链接的 PDF：

“总的来说，从工程角度来看，阿波罗废物管理系统的工作效果令人满意。然而，从机组人员接受度的角度来看，该系统必须给予较差的评分。尿液和粪便收集系统的主要问题是，这些系统需要比机组人员在地球环境中习惯的操作更多的事实，因此被发现令人反感。尿液容器组件代表了一种阻止机组人员处理尿液样本的尝试，但由于尿液经常溢出， “消毒”过程受到阻碍。

粪便收集系统带来了一系列更令人讨厌的问题。收集过程需要大量技巧，以防止粪便从收集袋中漏出，从而污染机组人员、他们的衣服或客舱表面。此外，由于系统使用的难度，粪便收集过程极其耗时。阿波罗 7 号宇航员估计正确完成该过程所需的时间为 45 分钟。*很难将粪便袋放置在适当的位置；由于常穿服装背面的翻盖形成的开口太小，无法轻松放置袋子，这一事实使情况变得更加复杂。**如前所述，需要揉捏袋子来分散杀菌剂。

*阿波罗 7 号日志中的条目由宇航员沃尔特·坎宁安 (Walter Cunningham) 撰写。

**后来阿波罗任务中常穿服装的配置进行了修改以纠正 这个问题。”

1： https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/19760005603/downloads/19...

More on what astronauts found “objectionable” and “distasteful” with Apollo's system, from the PDF linked in the OP (1):

"In general, the Apollo waste management system worked satisfactorily from an engineering standpoint. From the point of view of crew acceptance, however, the system must be given poor marks. The principal problem with both the urine and fecal collection systems was the fact that these required more manipulation than crewmen were used to in the Earth environment and were, as a consequence, found to be objectionable. The urine receptacle assembly represented an attempt to preclude crew handling of urine specimens but, because urine spills were frequent, the objective of “sanitizing” the process was thwarted.

The fecal collection system presented an even more distasteful set of problems. The collection process required a great deal of skill to preclude escape of feces from the collection bag and consequent soiling of the crew, their clothing, or cabin surfaces. The fecal collection process was, moreover, extremely time consuming because of the level of difficulty involved with use of the system. An Apollo 7 astronaut estimated the time required to correctly accomplish the process at 45 minutes.* Good placement of fecal bags was difficult to attain; this was further complicated by the fact that the flap at the back of the constant wear garment created an opening that was too small for easy placement of the bags.** As was noted earlier, kneading of the bags was required for dispersal of the germicide.

*Entry in the log of Apollo 7 by Astronaut Walter Cunningham.

**The configuration of the constant wear garments on later Apollo missions were modified to correct this problem."

1: https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/19760005603/downloads/19...

pbhjpbhj 2026/04/03 07:45
难道他们没有让宇航员事先在地球上模拟任务吗？穿衣服、吃饭、上厕所等等？

听起来这样可以让他们在出发前修好套装？

他们必须吃过饭菜之类的东西才能确保他们能够发挥作用，例如确保他们没有任何不耐受？

Did they not have the astronauts simulate the mission beforehand, on Earth? Wear the clothing, eat the meals, use the toilet, etc?

It sounds like that would have allowed them to fix the suit before they went?

They must have eaten the meals and such to be sure they could function, make sure they didn't have any intolerance, for example?

在 HN 中查看
smallmancontrov 2026/04/03 08:17
警告：恶心

当然可以，但根本问题是困难复合。首先：当没有重力持续拉扯粪便时，排便就会困难得多。地球上有点令人讨厌的东西（使用包，使用西装翻盖）当你有一个包，一个西装翻盖时，就会变成一场绝对的灾难， 和粪便分离失败。 现在你必须通过袋子和西装盖对背后你不想碰的物体进行精确的机械操作，每一次失败都会使工作量成倍增加， 现在粪便可以飘走，使您附近的工作量倍增。将抗菌剂揉捏到便便中也是如此：如果你在地球上未能彻底做到这一点，细菌排出的气体会导致袋子排气，但很可能这就是结束，因为你可以安排重力使便便远离排气口。事实上，您可能会这样做，甚至没有思考或想象它会如何出错。在零重力下，你不能简单地安排“通风口在上，粪便在下”，因此该事件很可能会将雾化的粪便发射到你的生活环境中，你在接下来的几天里必须忍受它。

在重力消失之前，很难完全理解它。

宇航员是他们所承担的风险的英雄，但他们也是处理问题的英雄 这。

Warning: gross

Of course, but the fundamental problem is that difficulties compound. It starts with: pooping is much harder when gravity isn't there to persistently tug on the turd. Something that is slightly obnoxious on Earth (using a bag, using a suit flap) turns into an absolute trainwreck when you have a bag, a suit flap, and turd separation failure. Now you have to do precise mechanical manipulation of an object you don't want to touch behind your back through a bag and a suit flap, every failure multiplies the work, and now the turds can float away to multiply the work outside your immediate vicinity. Ditto for kneading the antibacterial into the poo: if you fail to do this thoroughly on Earth, bacterial offgassing causes the bag to vent, but in all likelihood that's the end of it because you can arrange for gravity to keep the poo away from the vent. In fact, you would probably do this without even thinking or imagining how it could go wrong. In zero gravity, you can't simply arrange "vent on top, poo on bottom", so the event is likely to launch aerosolized poo into your living environment where you have to put up with it for the next few days.

It's difficult to fully appreciate gravity until it's gone.

Astronauts are heroes for the risks they take, but they are also heroes for dealing with this.

在 HN 中查看
asdff 2026/04/03 09:39
看来一个大问题是我猜出于文化原因坚持让这成为一个单独的行动。看起来如果有两名宇航员的话就很容易了。让一个弯下腰，用双手张开脸颊，另一个基本上在狗屎出来时把手伸进狗屎袋里，然后把它们擦干净。并不比护士每天的工作更糟糕。
Seems like a big issue is I'm guessing insistence on having this be a solo operation for cultural reasons. Seems like it would be easy with two astronauts. Have the one bend over and spread the cheeks wide with both hands, the other basically does the hand in the dog poop bag trick right as the poop is coming out and wipes them up after. No worse than what a nurse does every day for work.

malfist 2026/04/03 07:52
如何模拟地球上的零重力？
How do you simulate zero gravity on earth?
在 HN 中查看
__MatrixMan__ 2026/04/03 09:21
也许浮力可以成为一个不错的替代品，至少对于固体废物部分来说是这样。 我想象每次训练结束后，水深齐腰并冲洗整个浴室。 也许某种抹刀/橡胶扫帚可能有助于分离，再加上机器人抹刀清洁剂和消毒剂。 会有监视器和摄像头，这样你就可以校准你的目标。 那将是一个多么奇怪的工作日啊。
Perhaps buoyancy could be a decent substitute, at least for the solid waste part. I imagine being waist deep and flushing the entire bathroom after each training session. Maybe some kind of spatula/squeegee might assist with separation, coupled with a robotic spatula cleaner and sanitizer. There would be a monitor and cameras so you could calibrate your aim. What an odd workday that would be.

在 HN 中查看
throwaway173738 2026/04/03 09:59
浮力仅适用于重力。物体所受到的浮力与排出液体的重量大小相等且方向相反。没有重力，没有重量。
buoyancy only applies in gravity. The buoyant force on an object is equal to and opposite of the weight of the displaced fluid. No gravity, no weight.
在 HN 中查看
MichaelApproved 2026/04/03 08:05
减重飞机。又名呕吐彗星。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reduced-gravity_aircraft

Reduced gravity aircraft. AKA the Vomit Comet.

jryb 2026/04/03 08:10
你只能得到大约 30 秒的零重力。那是如何工作的呢？
You only get ~30 seconds of zero G. How would that work?

YeGoblynQueenne 2026/04/03 08:19
坚持三天。然后您就可以立即出发了。
Hold it in for three days. Then you're ready to go in a flash.

dlcarrier 2026/04/03 08:34
这可能会花费更长的时间。 更安全的选择是三杯浓咖啡和两块麸皮松饼。
That would probably make it take longer. A safer bet would be three really strong cups of coffee and two bran muffins.

在 HN 中查看
Alupis 2026/04/03 05:33
昨天晚上听现场直播——他们对厕所进行了大量的故障排除。这需要咨询整个专家团队，包括“厕所主管”。看起来它并没有将废物“冲”进收集袋或类似的东西中 - 但他们最终能够让它发挥作用。

我发现美国宇航局和宇航员用来交流的语言绝对搞笑——“是的，我们很兴奋并渴望立即开始液体处理！”

很高兴他们成功了 - 祝 Atemis II 任务好运！

Listening to the live stream yesterday evening - they performed a significant amount of troubleshooting for the toilet. This required consulting with a full team of experts, including a "Toilet Lead". It seems it wasn't "flushing" waste into the collection bag or something similar - but they were eventually able to get it working.

I found the language NASA and the astronauts used to communicate absolutely hilarious - "Yes, we're excited and eager to begin immediate fluid disposal!"

Glad they got it working - best of luck to Atemis II mission!

在 HN 中查看
sillysaurusx 2026/04/03 07:25
显然，他们让它工作的方法是给马桶重新通电。
Apparently the way they got it working was to power cycle the toilet.
在 HN 中查看
whackernews 2026/04/03 07:19
我喜欢这个。无论我们做什么，无论我们将人类的极限推向多远，我们仍然必须拉屎。
I love this. No matter what we do and how far we push the limits of humanity, we still have to shit.
在 HN 中查看
api 2026/04/03 10:29
热力学第二定律规定一切都会大便。

任何使用能量、做功和转化物质的生物都必须以某种形式排便。

我们不知道那里的生命会采取什么形式，但我们知道它会排便。

即使是后生物机器生命也会以工业废物、废热等形式排出。

由于第二定律，即使近乎完美的回收也只能是近乎完美，而不是完美，这意味着超高效的有机体或闭路循环生态系统或行业仍然会大便。只是不多。它还会释放出大量的废热，我猜这是一种粪便，因为质量和能量最终是相等的。

如果外面有由血浆或其他东西构成的奇怪生命，它就会拉屎。可能是带电粒子什么的。

2001 年的巨石？它拉屎了。不知何故。

The second law of thermodynamics dictates that everything poops.

Anything alive that is using energy and doing work and transforming matter must poop in some form or another.

We don’t know what form life out there might take, but we know it poops.

Even post biological machine life would poop in the form of industrial waste, waste heat, etc.

Even near perfect recycling can only be near perfect, not perfect, due to the second law, which means a super efficient organism or closed cycle ecosystem or industry will still poop. Just not much. It will also emit a ton of waste heat, which I guess is kind of poop since mass and energy are ultimately equivalent.

If there’s weird life out there made of plasma or something, it poops. Probably charged particles or something.

The monolith in 2001? It poops. Somehow.

在 HN 中查看
azalemeth 2026/04/03 06:02
上厕所真的太重要了作为脊椎受伤的人，直到出现问题之前，您真的不会意识到它有多重要。

显然，以前的系统的缺点之一是固体和液体排泄物的分离理想情况下需要有人将两种排泄物分开。显然，男性宇航员发现这比女性宇航员容易得多。阿耳忒弥斯的马桶可以同时处理这两种情况。

我仍然认为他们有适合太空行走的老式最大吸收性服装。 （CF https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximum_Absorbency_Garment）

Toileting is really fecking important. As someone with a spinal injury you really don't realise just how important until it goes wrong.

Apparently one of the down sides about the previous system was that the separation of solid and liquid excreta ideally required someone to separate their excretion of both kinds. Apparently this is something that male astronauts found much much easier than female ones. Artemis's toilet can handle both at the same time.

I still think they have the good old fashioned Maximum Absorbency Garment for space walks though. (CF https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximum_Absorbency_Garment)

在 HN 中查看
asdff 2026/04/03 09:41
离心机可以分离粪便和尿液
Centrifuge would separate that stool and urine
在 HN 中查看
amelius 2026/04/03 06:23
> 固体和液体排泄物的分离

本发明可用于畜牧业。

> separation of solid and liquid excreta

this invention might be of use in livestock farming.

在 HN 中查看
bluGill 2026/04/03 08:22
几十年来，畜牧农民一直这样做。然而，它们有非常不同的限制。 如果一种物质与另一种物质混合一点也没关系——事实上它们需要固体中有足够的水才能进行适当的分解。两者通常也被泵送，因此通常预计固体比实际固体更粘稠。 他们在某些阶段不需要太多的水，但他们有足够的空间容纳大型沉淀池（阅读重力对他们有用）。 他们还处理比太空任务更多的废物，因此他们需要一些高效/廉价的东西。
Livestock farmers have been doing this for decades. However they have very different constraints. It doesn't matter if a little of one gets mixed with the other - in fact they need enough water in the solids for proper decomposition. Both are normally pumped as well, so the solids are generally expected to be more a viscous liquid than actual solids. They don't want too much water in some stages, but they have plenty of room for a large setteling tank (read gravity works for them). They are also dealing with far more waste than a space mission, so they need something that is efficient/cheap at quantity.
在 HN 中查看
OJFord 2026/04/03 06:28
GP 说的是以前需要的，而不是发明的。新的可以处理混合物；不一定（大概不是？）通过分离它。
GP is saying that was previously required, not that it was invented. The new one can handle the mixture; not necessarily (presumably not?) by separating it.
在 HN 中查看
faster 2026/04/03 05:44
很久以前，我在航天飞机上工作了一个夏天，我们小组的管理员对厕所管道很着迷，所以她让工程师每周几次过来提供规格和图表。真实的故事：液体废物系统中有一个组件，称为“最后一滴夹管”。她为此笑了好几个星期。
I worked on the shuttle for a summer a long time ago, and my group's admin was obsessed with the toilet plumbing so she had engineers stopping by with specs and diagrams a few times per week. True story: there was a component in the liquid waste system called the "last drop pinch tube". She laughed about that for weeks.
在 HN 中查看
detourdog 2026/04/03 06:26
观看《2001》时，我最开心的一件事就是海伍德阅读厕所说明书。笑话是，我们已经进化到人类最基本的功能已经变得复杂的程度。

https://sites.google.com/site/theageofplastic3d/2001s-zero-g...

One of the good laughs I had watching 2001 was Haywood reading the instructions for the toilet. The joke being we have evolved to the point that our most basic human functions has become complex.

https://sites.google.com/site/theageofplastic3d/2001s-zero-g...

idatum 2026/04/03 07:19
海伍德焦急地读书！令人难忘的场景。
Haywood reads anxiously! Memorable scene.
在 HN 中查看
detourdog 2026/04/03 08:00
高风险情况:)
High stakes situation:)
在 HN 中查看
JumpCrisscross 2026/04/02 11:28
这是一部愚蠢、乏味的作品，它以一种只关注性感的部分的方式合法地促进了长期的太空探索野心， 例如 推进力。
This is one of those stupid, unglamorous works that legitimately facilitates long-term space exploration ambitions in a way just focusing on the sexy bits, e.g. propulsion.
在 HN 中查看
giantrobot 2026/04/03 06:39
我通过对厕所的关注来衡量所有载人太空探索计划的严肃性。如果厕所问题没有解决，而且可靠性很高，那么你只是在编写科学函数，根本没有认真对待实际的载人太空探索。厕所是载人航天提案中的棕色 M&M 条款[0]。

厕所极其不起眼，但绝对至关重要。人类制造危险且可能致命的废物。每天。它需要被安全地丢弃/收容。在微重力的密封环境中，它甚至比在地球上还要危险。

雾化的粪便物质可以进入肺部并引起致命的感染。进入消化系统可能导致衰弱（可能致命）的疾病。如果进入眼睛会暂时失明。如果它进入设备，还会导致机械或电气问题。所有这些都可能导致任务失败，在极端情况下甚至导致机组人员全部丧生。阿波罗 8 号非常幸运，弗兰克·博尔曼的病没有造成 更多的 问题。

如果您不考虑物流和基础设施，那么您就没有真正认真地对待一项工作。

[0] https://www.smithsonianmag.com/arts-culture/why-did-van-hale...

I gauge the seriousness of all manned space exploration proposals by the attention paid to the toilets. If the toilets are not a solved problem with many nines of reliability, you're just writing science function and are not at all serious about actual manned space exploration. Toilets are the brown M&M clause[0] of manned spaceflight proposals.

Toilets are unglamorous in the extreme but absolutely vital. Humans make hazardous and potentially deadly waste. Every day. It needs to be safely discarded/contained. In a sealed environment in microgravity it's even more dangerous than it is on Earth.

Aerosolized fecal matter can enter the lungs and cause deadly infections. Entering the digestive system can cause debilitating (possibly deadly) illness. Temporary blindness if it gets in the eyes. It can also cause mechanical or electrical problems if it gets in equipment. All of these can lead to a mission failure and in extreme instances a total loss of the crew. Apollo 8 was extremely lucky that Frank Borman's illness didn't cause more problems.

If you're not thinking logistics and infrastructure you're not really serious about an endeavor.

[0] https://www.smithsonianmag.com/arts-culture/why-did-van-hale...

在 HN 中查看
api 2026/04/03 10:32
说到这里：我们来谈谈空间结算。

如果你要留下来，你就会生孩子。

任何关于太空定居点（行星、月球、自转站等）的技术树提案如果不涉及如何制造、再利用或回收尿布，那么都是不严肃的。

我从来没有在科幻小说或太空书呆子关于定居火星之类的讨论中提到过这一点。那里有饮用水，至少如果你想让人类繁衍的话。

Speaking of this: let’s talk about space settlement.

If you’re going to stay, you are going to be having babies.

Any tech tree proposal for a space settlement (planet, moon, spin stations, whatever) that does not address how to make and reuse or recycle diapers is not serious.

I never see this mentioned in sci-fi or in space nerd discourse around stuff like what you need to settle Mars. It’s up there with potable water, at least if you want humans to reproduce.

在 HN 中查看
pyuser583 2026/04/03 08:52
坦率地说，地球也是如此。由于没有花足够的精力来明智地管理人类排泄物，许多人因此而死亡。
Frankly this is true about Earth too. Not enough effort is spent wisely managing human waste, and many people die as a result.
在 HN 中查看
mmooss 2026/04/03 06:59
我明白你的意思。出于好奇：

> 在微重力的密封环境中，甚至比在地球上还要危险。 / 雾化的粪便物质可以进入肺部并导致致命的感染。

空气过滤/回收系统能否最大限度地降低这种风险？

I see your point. Out of curiosity:

> In a sealed environment in microgravity it's even more dangerous than it is on Earth. / Aerosolized fecal matter can enter the lungs and cause deadly infections.

Would the air filtration / recycling system minimize this risk?

在 HN 中查看
giantrobot 2026/04/03 07:18
空气过滤实际上会 帮助 传播气溶胶，因为气流会将它们带过机舱，然后再被过滤器捕获。

高功率“消除气溶胶”模式将是需要设计（和测试）的基础设施之一。即便如此，像猎户座或龙这样的单舱航天器在某些气溶胶疏散模式处于活动状态时也没有地方可供机组人员露营。所以你需要围绕它设计一个完整的程序。

阿波罗常穿服装的部分需求是为了弥补指挥舱和 LEM 中功能的不足。对于长期任务来说，这样的事情是不切实际的，因此厕所（以及一般的废物处理）需要成为一个可靠解决的问题。

The air filtration will actually help spread aerosols because air currents will carry them through the cabin before they're captured by a filter.

A high power "eliminate aerosols" mode would be one of those infrastructure things that need to be designed (and tested). Even then a single compartment spacecraft like the Orion or Dragon wouldn't have anywhere for the crew to bivouac while some aerosol evacuation mode was active. So you'd want a whole procedure designed around it.

Part of the need for the Apollo Constant Wear Garments was to make up for the lack of faculties in the command module and LEM. Such a thing would be impractical for a long duration mission so toilets (and waste disposal in general) need to be a reliably solved problem.

在 HN 中查看
api 2026/04/03 10:40
我可以想象《苍穹浩瀚》中的腰带们只是穿上西装发泄。当然，只有当你有一堆罐装空气或通过船上裂解矿物质制成的东西时，这才有效。
I could imagine the belters in The Expanse just throwing on suits and venting. Of course that only works if you have a bunch of canned air or something that makes it by cracking minerals on board.
在 HN 中查看
mmooss 2026/04/03 07:42
好点。几个热点：

> 空气过滤实际上有助于传播气溶胶，因为气流会将它们带过机舱，然后再被过滤器捕获。

厕所设施可以输入/吸入空气过滤系统。无论如何，也许是明智的。

> 高功率“消除气溶胶”模式将是需要设计（和测试）的基础设施之一。

我预计这项技术在工业环境中已经成熟，尽管这当然与微重力和航天器有限的资源有很大不同。也许它存在于空间站上？这种背景似乎仍然有很大不同。

>像猎户座或龙这样的单舱航天器不会有任何地方供船员露营

他们穿着宇航服，但其外部可能需要净化。也许他们只是出去了，尽管让全体船员同时在飞船外面可能不是一个好主意！也许是在紧急情况下。

Good points. A few hot takes:

> The air filtration will actually help spread aerosols because air currents will carry them through the cabin before they're captured by a filter.

The toilet facilities could have input / suction into the air filtration system. Maybe wise anyway.

> A high power "eliminate aerosols" mode would be one of those infrastructure things that need to be designed (and tested).

I expect the technology is mature in industrial settings, though of course that is much different than microgravity and the constrained resources of the spacecraft. Maybe it exists on the space station? That context still seems significantly different.

> a single compartment spacecraft like the Orion or Dragon wouldn't have anywhere for the crew to bivouac

In their spacesuits, though their exteriors may need decontamination. Maybe they just go outside, though probably not a great idea to have the entire crew outside the spacecraft simultaneously! Maybe in an emergency.

在 HN 中查看
mememememememo 2026/04/03 05:20
这是管道，不是瓷器。
It is the plumbing, not the porcelain.

trhway 2026/04/03 06:24

a good attempt at popularization of the issue in Big Bang Theory

furyofantares 2026/04/03 05:54
在此之后，我刚刚收听了 NASA 的直播，我听到的第一件事也是唯一的事情是“我们成功弹射了。厕所可以使用了”
I just tuned into the NASA live stream after this and the first, and only, thing I've heard is "we've had a successful ejection. toilet is go for use"
在 HN 中查看
beloch 2026/04/03 07:01
减少废物处理的需要是 MRE 稳定饮食（有时称为“拒绝退出的膳食”）的好处之一。 令人清醒的是，任何曾经踏上月球的人在走出 LEM 时很可能都遇到了糟糕的情况。
Reduced need for waste disposal is one of the mixed blessings of a steady diet of MRE's (sometimes called "Meals Refusing to Exit"). It's sobering to realize that anyone who has ever set foot on the moon was most likely backed up in a bad way when they stepped out of their LEM.
在 HN 中查看
asdff 2026/04/03 09:44
他们不是有2000卡路里吗？我觉得我会向医生乞求泻药。一定感觉就像一列 5 英里长的货运列车卡在 1 英里长的隧道里。
Aren't they like 2000 calories? I feel like I would be begging the medic for laxatives. Must feel like a 5 mile freight train stuck in a 1 mile tunnel.

Marsymars 2026/04/03 08:04

clutter55561 2026/04/03 06:11

Joel_Mckay 2026/04/03 07:51

yanko 2026/04/03 08:04

There is interesting exact timing for (first attempt for sure) the noise of getting humans round trip around the moon, that space toilet discussion and the shitty situation with aircraft carriers in failed war with Iran.

caminanteblanco 2026/04/03 05:19

lorenzohess 2026/04/03 08:25

gcanyon 2026/04/03 05:40
他们应该把水管工训练成宇航员，而不是把宇航员训练成水管工。 （世界末日参考）

但说真的，尽管我想公平地说会发生错误，但仍然：他们无法正确安装管道？

They should have trained plumbers to be astronauts instead of training astronauts to be plumbers. (Armageddon reference)

But seriously, although I guess it’s fair to say that errors will occur, still: they couldn’t get the plumbing right?

joshstrange 2026/04/02 22:17
虽然我对太空一直很感兴趣，但我从来没有研究过厕所的情况，而且我的脑海中牢牢记住了一部不切实际的儿童电影中的场景 [0]，因为“这就是他们在太空中使用卫生间的方式，或者是因为那部电影已经很老了，所以更好的东西”。

[0] https://youtu.be/pJQGJmYKWZ0?t=131

While space has always interested me quite a bit, I've never looked into the toilet situation and I had this scene [0] from an unrealistic kids movie firmly fixed in my brain as "this is how they use the restroom in space, or something better since that movie is old".

fwipsy 2026/04/03 07:44
读到所有猎户座隔热罩工程问题真是太讽刺了，但至少他们有一个突破性的新厕所！
It's so ironic reading about all of the Orion heat shield engineering problems but at least they have a groundbreaking new toilet!!

spiderice 2026/04/03 10:02
是的，他们把工程师从隔热罩上拉下来来设计厕所。 那是 完全 它是如何运作的。
Yes they pulled engineers off the heat shield to engineer the toilet. That is totally how it works.
在 HN 中查看
convexly 2026/04/03 07:24
世界上所有先进的工程，你仍然需要弄清楚厕所在零重力下如何工作。
All the advanced engineering in the world and you still need to figure out how a toilet works in zero gravity.
在 HN 中查看
NetMageSCW 2026/04/03 08:03
我希望能与龙马桶的工作原理进行一些比较。
I wish there had been some comparison to how the Dragon toilet works.
在 HN 中查看
themafia 2026/04/03 05:42
> 航天飞机和国际空间站 (ISS) 上的早期厕所都使用这种真空系统

用于液体废物。 固体废物的情况并非如此。 实际上它只是一辆坦克。 它里面有一个像“网”一样的东西，它通过齿轮连接到一个轴，再连接到座椅前部的另一个轴。 每隔 7 天左右，指挥官就会“启动装置”旋转网，收集所有废物并将其压实到厕所的一侧。

许多指挥官表示，这是任务中压力最大的部分，因为该装置有些脆弱，很容易损坏。 在这种情况下，您必须戴上手套并手动完成网络本来要做的工作。

如果这完全失败了，是的，航天飞机在中间甲板的储物柜中存放了备用的“阿波罗袋”。

> Early toilets on both the space shuttle and the International Space Station (ISS) used this vacuum system

For liquid waste. This was not exactly the case for solid waste. Effectively it was just a tank. It had something like a "net" in it, this was connected to a shaft, through a gear, to another shaft at the front of the seat. The commander would, every 7 days or so, "actuate the mechanism" to rotate the net and to gather all the waste and compact it into one side of the toilet.

Many commanders said this was the most stressful part of the mission as the mechanism was somewhat delicate and could easily break. In that case you had to don a glove and manually do the work the net was otherwise doing.

If that completely failed, yes, the shuttle had backup "Apollo bags" stored in the middeck lockers.

在 HN 中查看
